source_metadata.title_raw: Karl H. Berger - A-18-247 - November 26, 2018 - Burbank

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Karl H. Berger 
District Counsel 
Hensley Law Group 
Burbank Office 
2600 W. Olive Avenue, Suite 500 
Burbank, CA 91505 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-247 
Dear Mr. Berger: 
November 26, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Lamont Public Utility 
District ("District") regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the 
"Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does any member of the District's Board of Directors have a conflict of interest that 
disqualifies them from participating in a Proposition 218 public hearing concerning a potential 
increase to water and wastewater rates? 
CONCLUSION 
All Directors have a conflict of interest under the Act regarding a potential increase to water 
and wastewater rates. The public generally exception applies, however, to allow them to participate 
in this decision. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 ofthe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-18-247 
Page No. 2 
You are District Counsel for the District and are making this request for advice on its behalf. 
On November 27, 2018, the District will be conducting a public hearing in accordance with Article 
XIII D, section 6 of the California Constitution and the Proposition 218 Omnibus Implementation 
Act (Government Code,§§ 53750, et seq.; collectively "Prop 218"). You are asking whether all five 
members on the District's Board of Directors ("Board") may participate in the public hearing._ 
The District serves the unincorporated communities of Lamont and Weedpatch located in 
Kem County, California. The District originally provided only potable water services when it was 
formed in 1943; in 1963, the District added wastewater collection and treatment services. Currently, 
The District provides water and wastewater services to a population of more than 18,000 people in a 
service area of approximately 5. 8 square miles. 
The District is governed by a five-member Board elected pursuant to Public Utilities Code 
("PUC") section 15956. In accordance with PUC section 15956, members for the Board are elected 
by office, at large. This means that each directorship is a separate office for purposes of nomination, 
election, and filling of vacancies (PUC, § 15955); the District has not opted for a "true" at-large 
election pursuant to Public Utilities Code section 15956(b ). Once elected to an office, each Director 
serves a four-year term. To qualify for office, a Director must be a resident and qualified elector 
within the District (PUC, § 15952). 
On March 6, 2018, the Board authorized the District's General Manager to commence Prop 
218 procedures to increase water and wastewater rates. A public hearing is scheduled for November 
27, 2018. On October 23, 2018, the Board adopted a resolution establishing procedures for 
conducting the Prop 218 public hearing. You note that the proposed rate increase would apply to all 
residential and commercial properties, and affect all ratepayers in the same manner as an individual 
Board member. 
You note that all Directors own their homes within the District, and that Directors Prado, 
Sanchez and Cruz own rental properties within the District boundaries, where the District provides 
potable water delivery and wastewater removal. You also note that Director Sanchez owns an 
automotive maintenance business to which the District provides services. 
Separately, Director Prado also serves on the Board of Trustees for the Vineland Elementary 
School District (the "School District"), a position she has held since December 2016. You note that 
members of the board of the School District do not receive remuneration for service, but that 
Director Prado does receive health benefits from the School District for her service. The School 
receives water and wastewater services from the District; it will be affected by any potential rate 
increase proposed by the District and may protest such increases in accordance with Prop 218. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the 
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 


File No. A-18-247 
Page No. 3 
effect on one or more of the •official's interests. Section 87103 also identifies the financial interests 
that may give rise to a conflict of interest. As pertinent to this request, interests from which a 
conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103 and include the following: 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
• 
Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) An "interest in real property" includes "any leasehold, 
beneficial or ownership interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real property 
located in the jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official, or 
other filer, or his or her immediate family if the fair market value of the interest is two 
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." (Section 82033.) 
• 
Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
• 
A public official's own personal finances, or those of a member of his or her immediate 
family. (Section 87103).) 
Source of Income - Director Prado 
You state that Director Prado receives health benefits from the School District. Thus, the 
School District is a source of income to her if she received $500 in "income" from the School 
District. However, the income Director Prado receives from the School District falls under an 
exception to the definition of income under the Act (Section 82030(b )(2)), which provides that 
when the employer who provides a salary to an official is a government agency, there is typically 
no economic interest because of the "government salary exception." 
Included in the government salary exception are fringe benefits that are part of a 
compensation package paid by a governmental agency such as pension benefits, health benefits, 
deferred compensation, tax sheltered annuities, life insurance, leaves, etc., which are considered by 
the Commission to be a part of the total salary in that the employee agrees to receive a smaller 
payment in exchange for receiving the remaining portion of his compensation by way of these 
fringe benefits. (Regulation 18232; In re Moore (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 33.) Because the School 
District is a local government agency under Section 82041, the income Director Prado receives 
from the School District is not considered "income" under Section 82030(b )(2) and therefore could 
not form the basis for a conflict-of-interest. 
Real Property and Business Interests 
All Directors own the homes in which they reside, located within the District. As such, all 
Directors have an interest in their real property residences (Section 87103(b)). Directors Prado, 
Sanchez, and Cruz each have an interest in residential rental properties (Section 87103(b)), and an 
interest in any tenant as a source of income from whom they each received at least $500 within 12 


File No. A-18-247 
Page No. 4 
months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c)). Director Sanchez has an interest in an automotive 
maintenance business (Section 87103(a) and (d)). 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's interest is presumed to be 
reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. Regulation 
18701(a) provides: 
"Financial Interest Explicitly Involved: A financial effect on a financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named 
party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the 
official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision 
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, 
or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any 
governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in 
Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6)." 
Additionally, Regulation 18702.2(a)(3) provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect of a governmental decision on real property is material whenever the governmental decision, 
"Would impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to the parcel." 
Accordingly, the financial effect o( a decision to increase water and wastewater rates will 
have foreseeable and material effect on the Directors' property and, absent an applicable exception, 
they would have a potentially disqualifying financial interest in the decision. 
Public Generally Exception 
Section 87103 prohibits an official from taking part in a decision only if the effect of the 
decision on the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
Regulation 18703(a) sets forth the general rule to determine if the effect of the decision on the 
official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial 
interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official 
establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or 
her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant 
segment." 
In most cases, the "public generally" exception, will apply only if the decision will affect a 
"significant segment of the public" defined as at least 25 percent of (1) the businesses or non-profit 
entities; (2) the real property, commercial real property, or residential real property; or (3) the 
individuals within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703 (b ). ) 


File No. A-18-247 
Page No. 5 
However, Regulation 18703( e) provides exceptions, which apply in special circumstances. 
As pertinent to your question, Regulation 18703(e)(l) provides an exception if the official 
establishes: 
"The decision establishes or adjusts assessments, taxes, fees, or rates for 
water, utility, or other broadly provided public services or facilities that are applied 
equally, proportionally, or by the same percentage to the official's interest and other 
businesses, properties, or individuals subject to the assessment, tax, fee, or rate." 
In this case, you stated the increase to water and wastewater rates would affect all ratepayers 
in the same manner as an individual Director. Because this decision concerns the establishment of 
taxes or "rates for water, utility, or other broadly provided public services" that will be applied 
proportionally to all properties, including the Directors' properties, the exception would apply. 
Accordingly, all five board members may participate in the Proposition 218 public hearing 
concerning a potential increase to water and wastewater rates. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Zachary W. Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 
ZWN:gal 
,.

sections.question:
Does any member of the District's Board of Directors have a conflict of interest that 
disqualifies them from participating in a Proposition 218 public hearing concerning a potential 
increase to water and wastewater rates?
sections.conclusion:
All Directors have a conflict of interest under the Act regarding a potential increase to water 
and wastewater rates. The public generally exception applies, however, to allow them to participate 
in this decision.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does any member of the District's Board of Directors have a conflict of interest that 
disqualifies them from participating in a Proposition 218 public hearing concerning a potential 
increase to water and wastewater rates?

CONCLUSION: All Directors have a conflict of interest under the Act regarding a potential increase to water 
and wastewater rates. The public generally exception applies, however, to allow them to participate 
in this decision.
